locked_facts: Focal length = R/2 where R = radius of curvature
Object beyond C: real, inverted, diminished image
Object at C: real, inverted, same size image at C
Object between C and F: real, inverted, magnified image
Object at F: image at infinity
Object within F: virtual, erect, magnified image behind mirror
truth_statements: A concave mirror converges light rays that are parallel to the principal axis to the focus
The mirror formula is 1/v + 1/u = 1/f where all distances are measured from the pole
When an object is placed within the focal length, the image is virtual, erect and magnified
Concave mirrors are used as shaving mirrors and in solar furnaces
The focal length of a concave mirror equals half the radius of curvature

aha_moment.what_to_show: Place object just inside F. Reflected rays diverge — they never meet in front of the mirror. But their backward extensions meet BEHIND the mirror, forming a virtual, erect, magnified image. This is exactly how a shaving/makeup mirror works — you hold your face within F to see a magnified virtual image.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Transition from real inverted image (object beyond F) to virtual erect magnified image (object within F). The crossover at F is the critical position.

simulation_states.label: Object beyond C — real, inverted, diminished
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Object far from mirror. Three reflected rays converge between F and C. Small inverted image.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: When the object is beyond C, the image is real, inverted, and smaller than the object.

simulation_states.label: Object between C and F — real, inverted, magnified
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Object between C and F. Reflected rays converge beyond C. Image is larger and inverted.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: Moving the object closer to F pushes the image farther away and makes it larger.

simulation_states.label: Object within F — virtual, erect, magnified
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Object between F and P. Reflected rays diverge. Virtual image (dotted) behind mirror — erect and magnified.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: This is the shaving mirror mode. Inside F, the concave mirror acts as a magnifying mirror.

simulation_states.label: Interactive — move the object
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Slider to adjust object distance. Watch image nature change as object crosses F.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: F is the critical boundary: beyond F = real inverted, within F = virtual erect magnified.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: A concave mirror always forms a real image because it converges light
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: A concave mirror forms a virtual image when the object is placed within the focal length. The reflected rays diverge and their backward extensions form a virtual, erect, magnified image behind the mirror.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show the object crossing F — the image flips from real (inverted, in front) to virtual (erect, behind mirror). This is why F is the most important position to remember.
